title: Wordy Phrases & Simplification Mistakes - 50 Mistakes
definition: Wordy phrases use many words where a single, simpler word would communicate the same meaning more clearly and directly. While not always grammatically 'wrong', these phrases make writing feel cluttered and less confident. Here are 50 common wordy phrases, explained simply, with a shorter, clearer alternative.

commonMistakes.wrong: At this point in time, we need to decide.
commonMistakes.right: Now, we need to decide.
commonMistakes.why: "At this point in time" is a wordy way of simply saying 'now' - the shorter word communicates the exact same meaning with much less clutter. Whenever you catch yourself writing 'at this point in time' or 'at the present time', replace it with 'now' or 'currently'.

commonMistakes.wrong: Due to the fact that it was raining, we stayed home.
commonMistakes.right: Because it was raining, we stayed home.
commonMistakes.why: "Due to the fact that" is a long-winded way of saying 'because' - the shorter word is clearer and more direct. Always simplify 'due to the fact that' to just 'because' in your writing.

commonMistakes.wrong: In the event that you need help, call me.
commonMistakes.right: If you need help, call me.
commonMistakes.why: "In the event that" is a formal, wordy substitute for the simple conditional word 'if'. Replace 'in the event that' with 'if' for clearer, more natural writing.

commonMistakes.wrong: In order to succeed, you must work hard.
commonMistakes.right: To succeed, you must work hard.
commonMistakes.why: "In order to" can almost always be shortened to just 'to' without losing any meaning. Whenever you write 'in order to', check if simply 'to' works just as well - it usually does.

commonMistakes.wrong: For the purpose of clarity, let me explain.
commonMistakes.right: To clarify, let me explain.
commonMistakes.why: "For the purpose of" is a wordy way of expressing intention that can usually be shortened to 'to' plus the relevant verb. Simplify 'for the purpose of clarity' to 'to clarify' for more direct writing.

commonMistakes.wrong: With regard to your question, I have an answer.
commonMistakes.right: Regarding your question, I have an answer. / About your question, I have an answer.
commonMistakes.why: "With regard to" can be shortened to just 'regarding' or 'about', saving words without losing meaning. Choose the shorter 'regarding' or 'about' instead of the longer 'with regard to'.

commonMistakes.wrong: In spite of the fact that he was tired, he finished the race.
commonMistakes.right: Although he was tired, he finished the race. / Despite being tired, he finished the race.
commonMistakes.why: "In spite of the fact that" is unnecessarily long - 'although' or 'despite' express the same contrast more directly. Replace this wordy phrase with the simpler 'although' or 'despite'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am of the opinion that this plan will work.
commonMistakes.right: I think this plan will work. / I believe this plan will work.
commonMistakes.why: "I am of the opinion that" is a formal, indirect way of simply saying 'I think' or 'I believe' - the shorter phrase is more direct and natural. Replace long opinion-introducing phrases with simple 'I think' or 'I believe'.

commonMistakes.wrong: It is important to note that the deadline is Friday.
commonMistakes.right: The deadline is Friday.
commonMistakes.why: "It is important to note that" often adds no real meaning - simply stating the fact directly is usually just as effective and much more concise. Consider removing this phrase entirely unless you specifically need to draw extra attention to something surprising.

commonMistakes.wrong: In the near future, we plan to expand.
commonMistakes.right: Soon, we plan to expand.
commonMistakes.why: "In the near future" is a wordy way of simply saying 'soon' - the shorter word communicates the same general timeframe. Replace 'in the near future' with 'soon' for more concise writing.

commonMistakes.wrong: Despite the fact that she was busy, she helped me.
commonMistakes.right: Although she was busy, she helped me. / Despite being busy, she helped me.
commonMistakes.why: "Despite the fact that" can be shortened to just 'although', or 'despite' followed directly by an -ing phrase, without the extra words. Simplify this phrase to either 'although' with a full clause, or 'despite' with an -ing phrase.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is the type of person who is always honest.
commonMistakes.right: He is always honest.
commonMistakes.why: "The type of person who" often adds unnecessary words when you can describe the quality directly. Consider whether you can state the quality directly without this wordy introductory phrase.

commonMistakes.wrong: Whether or not you agree, we must proceed.
commonMistakes.right: Whether you agree or not, we must proceed. / Regardless of your opinion, we must proceed.
commonMistakes.why: "Whether or not" is sometimes considered slightly redundant since 'whether' alone can imply the 'or not' possibility - though this phrase is very common and widely accepted. For tighter writing, consider 'whether you agree' alone, or 'regardless' as an alternative.

commonMistakes.wrong: I would like to take this opportunity to thank you.
commonMistakes.right: Thank you.
commonMistakes.why: "I would like to take this opportunity to" is a very formal, wordy introduction that can almost always be removed - simply stating your thanks directly is just as polite and much clearer. Remove this entire introductory phrase unless you're writing a very formal speech or ceremony.

commonMistakes.wrong: It goes without saying that honesty is important.
commonMistakes.right: Honesty is important.
commonMistakes.why: If something "goes without saying", then by definition, you don't need to say it - this phrase is often unnecessary filler before an obvious statement. Consider removing this phrase entirely, or only use it when genuinely emphasizing something everyone would agree with.

commonMistakes.wrong: Each and every one of us must contribute.
commonMistakes.right: Each of us must contribute. / Every one of us must contribute.
commonMistakes.why: "Each and every" combines two words with very similar meaning - choose just one for a cleaner, less repetitive sentence (this phrase is common for emphasis, but represents redundancy in careful writing). Pick either 'each' or 'every', not both together, unless deliberately using it for rhetorical emphasis.

commonMistakes.wrong: The reason is because I was busy.
commonMistakes.right: The reason is that I was busy. / I was busy.
commonMistakes.why: "The reason is because" repeats the explanatory function twice - 'reason' and 'because' both introduce a cause, so combining them is redundant. Use either 'the reason is that' or simply state the reason directly, without both words together.

commonMistakes.wrong: Absolutely essential requirements must be met.
commonMistakes.right: Essential requirements must be met.
commonMistakes.why: "Essential" already means absolutely necessary, so adding "absolutely" in front repeats the same emphasis unnecessarily. Trust that 'essential' alone conveys strong necessity without needing reinforcement.

commonMistakes.wrong: Past history shows that this strategy works.
commonMistakes.right: History shows that this strategy works.
commonMistakes.why: "History" by definition refers to the past, so adding "past" in front is redundant. Simply say 'history' alone, since it inherently refers to past events.

commonMistakes.wrong: Future plans include expanding the business.
commonMistakes.right: Plans include expanding the business.
commonMistakes.why: "Plans" already implies something for the future, so adding "future" in front is often unnecessary repetition (though sometimes used for extra clarity when contrasting with past plans). Consider whether 'future' adds real distinguishing information, or if 'plans' alone is sufficient.

commonMistakes.wrong: Basic fundamentals of grammar are important.
commonMistakes.right: Fundamentals of grammar are important.
commonMistakes.why: "Fundamentals" already means the basic, essential parts of something, so adding "basic" in front repeats the same idea. Simply say 'fundamentals' alone, without 'basic' in front.

commonMistakes.wrong: Final outcome of the match surprised everyone.
commonMistakes.right: The outcome of the match surprised everyone.
commonMistakes.why: "Outcome" already refers to the final result, so adding "final" in front is redundant. Trust that 'outcome' alone communicates the final result without needing extra emphasis.

commonMistakes.wrong: Close proximity to the office is convenient.
commonMistakes.right: Proximity to the office is convenient.
commonMistakes.why: "Proximity" already means closeness, so adding "close" in front repeats the same meaning. Simply say 'proximity' alone, since it inherently describes something close by.

commonMistakes.wrong: Unexpected surprise awaited us at the party.
commonMistakes.right: A surprise awaited us at the party.
commonMistakes.why: "Surprise" already implies something unexpected, so adding "unexpected" in front is redundant. Trust that 'surprise' alone conveys the unexpected nature of the event.

commonMistakes.wrong: End result of the experiment was positive.
commonMistakes.right: The result of the experiment was positive.
commonMistakes.why: "Result" already refers to the end product of a process, so adding "end" in front repeats the same idea. Simply say 'result' alone, without the unnecessary 'end' in front.

commonMistakes.wrong: Repeat again what you just said.
commonMistakes.right: Repeat what you just said. / Say that again.
commonMistakes.why: "Repeat" already means to say again, so combining it with "again" duplicates the same meaning. Choose either 'repeat' or 'say...again', not both stacked together.

commonMistakes.wrong: Advance planning is essential for success.
commonMistakes.right: Planning is essential for success.
commonMistakes.why: "Planning" by nature happens in advance (before something occurs), so adding "advance" in front is often unnecessary. Consider whether 'planning' alone communicates your point just as clearly.

commonMistakes.wrong: Free gift included with your purchase.
commonMistakes.right: Gift included with your purchase.
commonMistakes.why: "Gift" already implies something given without charge, so adding "free" in front repeats the same meaning. Trust that 'gift' alone conveys the concept of receiving something at no cost.

commonMistakes.wrong: New innovation in the field of medicine.
commonMistakes.right: Innovation in the field of medicine.
commonMistakes.why: "Innovation" already means something new, so adding "new" in front is redundant. Simply say 'innovation' alone, without 'new' repeating the same idea.

commonMistakes.wrong: Actual facts show the plan will fail.
commonMistakes.right: Facts show the plan will fail.
commonMistakes.why: "Facts" are inherently actual and true by definition, so adding "actual" in front repeats this quality unnecessarily. Trust that 'facts' alone communicates truth and accuracy without needing reinforcement.

commonMistakes.wrong: Close scrutiny of the report revealed errors.
commonMistakes.right: Scrutiny of the report revealed errors.
commonMistakes.why: "Scrutiny" already implies a careful, close examination, so adding "close" in front repeats the same idea. Simply say 'scrutiny' alone, since it inherently means careful examination.

commonMistakes.wrong: Empty space needs to be filled on the page.
commonMistakes.right: Space needs to be filled on the page.
commonMistakes.why: "Space" in this context already implies emptiness (unused area), so adding "empty" in front is often redundant. Consider whether 'space' alone communicates your meaning clearly enough.

commonMistakes.wrong: Sudden impulse made her buy the dress.
commonMistakes.right: An impulse made her buy the dress.
commonMistakes.why: "Impulse" already implies a sudden urge, so adding "sudden" in front repeats the same quality. Trust that 'impulse' alone conveys the spontaneous, quick nature of the action.

commonMistakes.wrong: Basic essentials for camping include a tent.
commonMistakes.right: Essentials for camping include a tent.
commonMistakes.why: "Essentials" already refers to basic, necessary items, so adding "basic" in front is redundant. Simply say 'essentials' alone, since it already implies the basic necessities.

commonMistakes.wrong: Completely eliminate all traces of the error.
commonMistakes.right: Eliminate all traces of the error.
commonMistakes.why: "Eliminate" already means to completely remove something, so adding "completely" in front repeats the same emphasis. Trust that 'eliminate' alone conveys total removal without needing reinforcement.

commonMistakes.wrong: Continue to remain calm during the emergency.
commonMistakes.right: Remain calm during the emergency.
commonMistakes.why: "Remain" already implies continuing in a state, so adding "continue to" in front repeats the same idea. Simply say 'remain calm' alone, without the redundant 'continue to'.

commonMistakes.wrong: Final conclusion of the report is promising.
commonMistakes.right: The conclusion of the report is promising.
commonMistakes.why: "Conclusion" already refers to the final part of something, so adding "final" in front is redundant. Trust that 'conclusion' alone communicates the ending point without needing 'final' added.

commonMistakes.wrong: Mutual cooperation between the two teams helped.
commonMistakes.right: Cooperation between the two teams helped.
commonMistakes.why: "Cooperation" already implies working together mutually, so adding "mutual" in front repeats the same idea. Simply say 'cooperation' alone, since it inherently involves both sides.

commonMistakes.wrong: Postpone until later since we're not ready.
commonMistakes.right: Postpone since we're not ready.
commonMistakes.why: "Postpone" already means to delay until a later time, so adding "until later" repeats the same meaning. Trust that 'postpone' alone conveys the delay without needing 'until later' added.

commonMistakes.wrong: Completely surrounded on all sides by fans.
commonMistakes.right: Surrounded by fans.
commonMistakes.why: "Surrounded" already means enclosed on all sides, so adding both "completely" and "on all sides" doubles up the same meaning twice. Simply say 'surrounded' alone, since it already implies being enclosed from every direction.

commonMistakes.wrong: Various different opinions were shared at the meeting.
commonMistakes.right: Various opinions were shared at the meeting. / Different opinions were shared at the meeting.
commonMistakes.why: "Various" and "different" both suggest multiple, distinct options, so combining them together is somewhat repetitive. Choose just one of these words, not both stacked together.

commonMistakes.wrong: Combine together the two mixtures.
commonMistakes.right: Combine the two mixtures.
commonMistakes.why: "Combine" already means to bring together, so adding "together" afterward repeats the same idea. Simply say 'combine' alone, without the redundant 'together'.

commonMistakes.wrong: Merge together the two departments.
commonMistakes.right: Merge the two departments.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'combine', "merge" already implies bringing things together, making "together" afterward unnecessary repetition. Trust that 'merge' alone communicates the joining of the two departments.

commonMistakes.wrong: Connect together these two wires.
commonMistakes.right: Connect these two wires.
commonMistakes.why: "Connect" already means to join together, so adding "together" afterward duplicates the same meaning. Simply say 'connect' alone, since it inherently means joining things.

commonMistakes.wrong: Final destination of our journey is Paris.
commonMistakes.right: The destination of our journey is Paris.
commonMistakes.why: "Destination" already refers to the final point of a journey, so adding "final" in front repeats the same meaning. Trust that 'destination' alone communicates the endpoint without needing 'final' added.

commonMistakes.wrong: Blend together the ingredients thoroughly.
commonMistakes.right: Blend the ingredients thoroughly.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'combine' and 'merge', "blend" already means to mix together, so "together" afterward is unnecessary repetition. Simply say 'blend' alone, since it already implies mixing multiple things.

commonMistakes.wrong: Unite together as one team.
commonMistakes.right: Unite as one team.
commonMistakes.why: "Unite" already means to come together as one, so adding "together" afterward repeats the same meaning. Trust that 'unite' alone conveys the joining together without needing 'together' added.

commonMistakes.wrong: The various different reasons for the delay.
commonMistakes.right: The various reasons for the delay. / The different reasons for the delay.
commonMistakes.why: This repeats the 'various/different' redundancy covered earlier - choose just one word to describe multiple distinct reasons. Keep practicing this specific pair until choosing just one feels automatic.

commonMistakes.wrong: In my personal opinion, I think this is wrong.
commonMistakes.right: In my opinion, this is wrong. / I think this is wrong.
commonMistakes.why: "In my personal opinion" and "I think" both introduce your view, so combining them together repeats the same function unnecessarily. Choose just one way to introduce your opinion, not both stacked together in the same sentence.

commonMistakes.wrong: The end result and final outcome were the same.
commonMistakes.right: The result was clear.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence stacks up multiple redundant phrases together (end result, final outcome) that all mean essentially the same thing - simplify to just one clear word. When revising your writing, look for multiple similar words clustered together and reduce them to just one clear choice.

quiz.question: Which is the simplest, clearest way to say 'at this point in time'?
quiz.explanation: 'Now' communicates the same meaning as 'at this point in time' with far fewer words.

quiz.sentence: Due to the fact that it was raining, we stayed home.
quiz.correction: Because it was raining, we stayed home.
quiz.explanation: 'Due to the fact that' is a wordy substitute for the simple word 'because'.

quiz.question: Which phrase is redundant (repeats the same meaning)?
quiz.explanation: 'Essential' already means absolutely necessary, so adding 'absolutely' repeats the same emphasis.

quiz.question: ___ to succeed, you must work hard. (shorter than 'in order to')
quiz.explanation: 'In order to' can almost always be shortened to just 'to'.

quiz.question: Which is the clearest way to express 'in the event that'?
quiz.explanation: 'If' is the simple, clear equivalent of the wordy phrase 'in the event that'.
